title: Universal Bound for Entanglement Generation
authors: Alfred Li, Daisuke Miki, Yanbei Chen
abstract: We derive a universal condition for entanglement generation under general bilinear interactions in the presence of white thermal noise. While various protocols have been proposed to enhance the amount of generated entanglement, it remains unclear whether they can also relax the threshold for entanglement generation itself. Using a Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad description, we analyze general multimode systems and derive a separability-preserving condition for bilinear interactions under white thermal noise. As an application to gravity-induced entanglement, we show that the gravitational interaction must dominate over thermal noise for entanglement to arise. In particular, this bound cannot be relaxed by changing the initial state or by introducing mediator systems, although such ingredients may enhance the amount of entanglement once it is generated. These results establish a general limitation on entanglement-generation protocols in thermal environments.
summary: This paper establishes a universal bound that prevents entanglement from being generated if thermal noise exceeds a specific threshold relative to the interaction strength. Crucially, it proves that this threshold cannot be bypassed by using mediators, changing initial states, or increasing the number of modes. This provides a fundamental limitation for quantum sensing and entanglement-based protocols in thermal environments.
main_problem: Determining whether various protocols, such as using mediators or changing initial states, can relax the fundamental threshold for entanglement generation in multi-mode Gaussian systems subject to thermal noise.
main_result: The authors derive a universal, state-independent bound for entanglement generation: for rank-1 interactions, separability is preserved if the product of thermal noise power spectra exceeds the square of the coupling strength ($S_{th,A} S_{th,B} \geq K_g^2$).
method: The study uses the Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad (GKSL) formalism, covariance matrix evolution via Langevin equations, and the construction of LOCC protocols using Gaussian weak measurements and feedback.
model_or_system: General multi-mode Gaussian (and some non-Gaussian) systems subject to bilinear interactions and white thermal noise, with a specific application to gravity-induced entanglement between two masses.
key_observables: Covariance matrix, entanglement (via PPT criterion), and thermal noise power spectra.
important_parameters: Gravitational coupling strength (Kg), thermal noise spectra (Sth), mechanical dissipation rate (gamma), and temperature (T).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes white thermal noise (delta-correlated in time), the Born-Markov approximation, and works in a perturbative regime where damping/friction effects are neglected.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the configuration of rank-1 coupled oscillators; Figure 2 illustrates general bilinear coupling with gravity and system-bath links; Figure 3 visualizes a tripartite system with a massive mediator.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of entanglement thresholds, moves to deriving the universal bound for rank-1 and general bilinear interactions using covariance matrices, extends the result to correlated noise, proves the robustness against non-Gaussianity and mediators, and concludes by constructing LOCC protocols that reproduce the target dynamics.
why_interesting: It establishes a fundamental, unrelaxable limit on entanglement generation in noisy environments, which is critical for researchers designing gravity-induced entanglement experiments or any open-system quantum protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Configuration of rank-1 bilinearly coupled systems: n oscil- lators in subsystem A (pink) couple to n oscillators in subsystem B (blue) via a rank-1 bilinear interaction Kg(ˆξT AuA)(ˆξT BuB) (thick line). Each subsystem couples linearly to its own thermal bath (orange and green, thin lines).

FIG. 3. A tripartite system involving entanglement generation be- tween subsystems A and B mediated by a massive mediator C. By combining subsystems B and C into a composite subsystem, we have reduced the problem to one of bipartite entanglement: as long as ther- mal noise levels of A and C are strong enough there can never be any entanglement generated between subsystems A and B.
FIG. 2. Systems with general bilinear coupling, with thicker lines representing gravity and thinner lines, system-bath couplings.
